metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the behavioral difference between `overlap_drain = "async"` and `overlap_drain = "always"` in the What section (e.g., async=opportunistic gate-checked detach, always=unconditional spawn on every invocation).
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain` STRING enum declares three values (`off|async|always`) but only `off` is behaviorally defined. The plan never explains what differentiates `async` from `always`, so an executor cannot implement the branch logic for these two non-off values.
findings.location: What section: '`overlap_drain` is a STRING enum (`off|async|always`)'; AC5: 'REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN=off disables'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line behavioral spec per value, e.g.: `off` = disabled; `async` = opportunistic (spawn only when soaked entries exist, respects gate budget); `always` = unconditional spawn on every write-command invocation regardless of gate check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value of `overlap_drain` explicitly in the Constants or What section (e.g., 'default: `async`') so `LLMConfig` initialization is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The default value of `overlap_drain` is never stated. The plan shows `overlap_drain = "off"` as the opt-out setting but does not declare what the shipped default is, so an executor cannot initialize `LLMConfig` correctly.
findings.location: What section: 'Opt-out: `overlap_drain = "off"` on `LLMConfig`'; Constants section; Scope: 'config fields `overlap_drain`/… on `LLMConfig`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Constants: `overlap_drain` default = `"async"` (or `"off"` if the feature is meant to be opt-in). One word resolves the ambiguity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test (e.g., `test_prune_after_drain`) to the Testing section and reference it in AC6 to prove that queue events are pruned after each drain cycle and `DONE_ENRICH` is appended.
findings.finding: The final sentence of AC6 — 'Each drain appends `DONE_ENRICH` + prunes so queue-event growth is bounded' — is a completion-relevant claim with no named proving command or test in either the Testing section or the ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 final sentence: 'Each drain appends DONE_ENRICH + prunes so queue-event growth is bounded'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Testing section with: `test_prune_after_drain` (after one `--once` cycle, assert the ticket's queue file contains exactly one cycle of events and a DONE_ENRICH entry). Reference `::test_prune_after_drain` in AC6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (intercept arm for `rebar enrich`) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` to the Scope's list of files to edit/create.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope omits `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` as a required edit target. The `rebar enrich` subcommand needs an intercept arm in `main()` (analogous to the `review`, `workflow`, `llm`, `criteria` intercepts). Without it, `main()` will reject `enrich` as unknown before reaching `_dispatch`. The `known_subcommands()` function in `_help.py` is driven entirely by `.txt` files in `src/rebar/_cli/help/`, so a missing `enrich.txt` also means `rebar enrich --help` silently falls through as 'unknown subcommand'.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py / src/rebar/_cli/help/
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add: `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (add `if argv and argv[0] == 'enrich': return _enrich(argv[1:])` intercept, import `_enrich` from `_llm_commands`); `src/rebar/_cli/help/enrich.txt` (pinned help text, per the _help.py contract).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the enforcement mechanism for `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`: either (a) `maybe_drain()` wraps the soaked?-check in a wall-clock timer and skips spawning if the check itself exceeds the budget, or (b) explicitly state the 20ms is a design-by-construction upper bound on the reducer's complexity class (e.g., O(1) via a cached counter), with no runtime abort needed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20` and the AC says `maybe_drain` no-ops "within `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`", but nowhere does the plan describe HOW this budget is enforced. If `pending_enrichment(now)` (the queue reducer call that performs the soaked?-check) takes longer than 20ms on a large queue, it is unclear whether `maybe_drain()` must abort the check and skip spawning, abort and spawn anyway, or whether the 20ms is simply a design-by-construction expectation with no runtime enforcement. The test `test_gate_latency` verifies the no-op case completes within budget, but without a specified enforcement mechanism the test could pass by coincidence.
findings.location: ## Constants; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (`test_gate_latency`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for (a) `rebar enrich --once` (processes exactly one soaked+unclaimed entry in-process, verified by `test_once`) and (b) `rebar enrich status` (outputs valid JSON with keys `pending`, `claimed`, `soaking`, each an integer).
findings.finding: The `--once` CLI flag (described in What: 'claim+process one entry, in-process, tests') and the `status` subcommand (described in What: 'prints JSON `{"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int}` from the queue reducer') are both named deliverables, and `test_once` appears in the Testing section, but neither has a corresponding Acceptance Criterion. The AC set covers only `--drain`, `maybe_drain`, lock contention, enrich failure/self-heal, key-absent/opt-out, and Windows detach.
findings.location: What section (CLI `rebar enrich`), Testing section (test_once), Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (e.g. `test_gate_spawns_child`) that stubs `subprocess.Popen` and asserts it is called with the correct POSIX kwargs (`start_new_session=True`, `close_fds=True`) and the right command (`rebar enrich --drain`) when at least one soaked+unclaimed entry exists; cover the POSIX branch analogously to `test_windows_detach_kwargs`.
findings.finding: The positive-spawn path of `maybe_drain` — when soaked+unclaimed entries exist and a detached child IS spawned — has no named test. Only the no-op (nothing soaked) path is covered by `test_gate_latency`. The AC for `maybe_drain` cites only `test_gate_latency`, which asserts no spawn occurs; no test verifies that a spawn is actually triggered, that the subprocess is called with the right arguments on POSIX, or that the child command line targets `rebar enrich --drain`.
findings.location: Testing section / AC line 2: 'maybe_drain no-ops … detaches a surviving child otherwise; ::{test_gate_latency}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_gate_spawns_child` in `tests/unit/test_enrich_drain.py`: mock `subprocess.Popen` + a queue stub returning one soaked entry, call `maybe_drain()`, assert `Popen` called once with `start_new_session=True` on POSIX and argv ending in `['rebar', 'enrich', '--drain']`. Mirror the existing `test_windows_detach_kwargs` structure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test for `rebar enrich status` (e.g. `test_status_output`) asserting the JSON output includes `pending`, `claimed`, and `soaking` integer fields; add an empty-queue variant asserting all-zeros output.
findings.finding: `rebar enrich status` is a new user-facing CLI subcommand (printing JSON `{"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int}`) with zero named tests anywhere in the plan. No happy-path, no empty-queue, no malformed-queue scenario is mentioned.
findings.location: What section: 'status (prints JSON {"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int} from the queue reducer)'; Testing section (absence of any status test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_status_output` and `test_status_empty_queue` in `tests/unit/test_enrich_drain.py`: invoke the status command against a FakeQueue with known counts and assert `json.loads(output) == {"pending": N, "claimed": M, "soaking": K}`; also invoke against an empty queue and assert all fields are 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC and a `test_status` test for `rebar enrich status`, or move it to a follow-on story (Tier-2) and remove it from this plan's Scope and CLI definition.
findings.finding: The `rebar enrich status` subcommand (prints JSON `{"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int}`) has no Acceptance Criterion and no test listed in the Testing section. It is a proposed API surface with zero current done-definition coverage — YAGNI.
findings.location: ## What — CLI `rebar enrich` definition; ## Testing; ## Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the behavioral meaning of ALL three `overlap_drain` enum values (`off`, `async`, `always`) — e.g. `off`=disabled, `async`=detach when soaked, `always`=detach unconditionally (bypassing the soaked?-check) — co-located with the enum declaration in the What section.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain` STRING enum declares three values (`off|async|always`) but only `off` is behaviorally defined. The plan never explains what differentiates `async` from `always`, so an implementor of `enrich_drain.py` cannot determine whether `always` means 'run synchronously in-process', 'always spawn even when the soaked?-check is false', or something else entirely.
findings.location: What section: "`overlap_drain` is a STRING enum (`off|async|always`) so it lives on `LLMConfig`"
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`off` — drain never runs; `async` — detach only when the soaked?-check finds pending entries (the default opportunistic mode); `always` — always detach a drain child regardless of the soaked?-check result (useful for CI pre-warm).' Mirror this in the `LLMConfig` field docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the naming conflict: state the canonical Python attribute names (`overlap_drain_batch`, `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`), their corresponding env vars (`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN_BATCH`, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`), and clarify whether `DRAIN_BATCH` / `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` are module-level aliases or the attribute names themselves.
findings.finding: The plan uses two inconsistent naming schemes for the same two numeric config fields: the Constants section calls them `DRAIN_BATCH` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` (module-level constants), while the Scope section calls them `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` (LLMConfig attribute names). Neither section resolves which is the Python attribute name on `LLMConfig` vs which is a local constant alias, and no env var names for these two fields are stated.
findings.location: Constants section: 'DRAIN_BATCH = 5, DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20 — both on LLMConfig'; Scope section: 'config fields overlap_drain/overlap_drain_batch/overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms on LLMConfig'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the constant names in the Constants section (`DRAIN_BATCH`, `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`) with the config field names in Scope (`overlap_drain_batch`, `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`) — either state that the constants are Python-level aliases for the config fields, or rename one set to match the other throughout.
findings.finding: The Constants section names the two numeric config fields `DRAIN_BATCH` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` ('both on `LLMConfig`'), while the Scope section names the same fields `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` on `LLMConfig`. These are the only names given in either section; no aliasing or derivation relationship is explained. An implementer reading both sections would not know whether to define one name, two names, or a constant derived from a config field.
findings.location: Constants section vs Scope section (config fields on LLMConfig)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Constants section, change to: '`overlap_drain_batch` (default 5) and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` (default 20) — config fields on `LLMConfig`; referenced as `cfg.overlap_drain_batch` / `cfg.overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` in code.' Update AC wording to match the config field names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or fix) that `transition`, `reopen`, `claim`, and `compact` call `push_after_commit` directly and bypass the `maybe_drain()` hook in `write_and_push`; decide whether to also inject `maybe_drain()` into `push_after_commit` or document the intentional omission.
findings.finding: The plan's `maybe_drain()` hook is placed exclusively inside `write_and_push` in `event_append.py`, but three inline-commit write paths — `transition`/`reopen`, `claim`, and `compact` — commit via `txn.transition_core`/`claim_core`/the compact writer and then call `push_after_commit` (in `push.py`) directly, NOT through `write_and_push`. These paths are high-frequency write commands that will never trigger `maybe_drain()`. The plan names `event_append` as the 'single authoritative site' but does not enumerate these unenumerated consumers or acknowledge they won't trigger the drain.
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point (decided — no 'or') section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document explicitly why `push_after_commit` (used by transition, compact, claim, delete) is excluded from the `maybe_drain()` injection point, and confirm that enrichment queues draining only on event-append invocations is acceptable scope for Tier-1.
findings.finding: The plan states `maybe_drain()` is the "single authoritative site" and is placed only in `event_append.write_and_push`, but the codebase has a second push path — `push.push_after_commit` (push.py:267-284) — used by inline-commit commands: `transition`, `compact`, `claim`, and `delete`. These commands write to the store but never call `write_and_push`, so `maybe_drain()` never fires from them. The plan does not explain this exclusion or acknowledge that a project with only transition/compact/claim activity would never opportunistically drain. This gap between the 'single authoritative site' claim and the actual coverage should be explicitly scoped.
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point; ## Scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `pending_enrichment(now)`'s module path (which S3 file), its call signature and return type (e.g. `list[str]` of ticket IDs, or `int` count), and confirm that a cheap 'any pending?' boolean variant is available for the maybe_drain gate check that satisfies DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS.
findings.finding: The `pending_enrichment(now)` reducer — the predicate at the heart of the soaked?-check gate and the drainer loop — has no specified module path, return type, or schema. The plan references it by name in the Drainer loop section ('from `pending_enrichment(now)` PLUS the fallback scan') but it does not exist in any current codebase file (search confirms absence), and the plan does not say which file it will be defined in, what it returns, or how it is cheaply callable within DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS.
findings.location: Drainer loop section: 'from `pending_enrichment(now)` PLUS the fallback scan (tickets certified but with absent/stale digest'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state after prune commits: specify that rolled-back code will still find the store consistent (DONE_ENRICH events persist, pruned queue events are intentionally gone), document that pruned events are unrecoverable (compensate-forward only), and add a recovery step for a prune interrupted between `git rm` and `git commit` (e.g., `git reset HEAD` on next drain startup).
findings.finding: The plan's `prune` step (git rm + commit of old queue events) is destructive and irreversible, but the plan never asserts the post-rollback schema state after a prune has executed. If code is rolled back after prune commits have been made, the pruned events are permanently gone from git history. Furthermore, if a prune is interrupted between the `git rm` (staging) and the `git commit` step, the events are staged-deleted but not committed — an intermediate state the plan does not address.
findings.location: What section — 'prune the ticket's queue events (keep the latest cycle) so log growth is bounded'; AC — 'Each drain appends DONE_ENRICH + prunes so queue-event growth is bounded'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan note: 'Prune is compensate-forward only — pruned queue events are not recoverable on rollback; this is acceptable because DONE_ENRICH and TICKET_DIGEST are the authoritative state. The drain startup checks for a dirty git index (staged-but-uncommitted deletions from a prior interrupted prune) and either commits or resets them before proceeding.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `maybe_drain()` is wrapped in a broad try/except in `event_append.py` so any exception from the soaked?-check or spawn silently logs and returns without propagating to the parent write command.
findings.finding: The `maybe_drain()` call in `event_append.py` is in the hot path of all ordinary write commands, but the plan does not specify that it is wrapped in a try/except to protect the parent process if the soaked?-check itself throws (e.g., I/O error reading the queue, permissions issue, or unexpected exception from the queue reducer).
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point / `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'maybe_drain() is wrapped in `try/except Exception` in event_append — any failure (I/O error, spawn failure, queue read error) is logged at DEBUG level and returns silently; it NEVER raises to the caller.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified fallback for `prune` failure after `DONE_ENRICH` succeeds: e.g. 'log the prune error and continue the batch; a future drain will retry prune on re-inspection of the DONE_ENRICH event'.
findings.finding: The fallback for a `prune` failure after a successful `DONE_ENRICH` append is unspecified. The per-item error handling paragraph covers only `enrich()` exceptions; it says nothing about what happens if `prune` throws after DONE_ENRICH has been committed — a case where the ticket is correctly marked done but queue-event growth remains unbounded.
findings.location: Drainer loop section: 'append `DONE_ENRICH`, then `prune` the ticket's queue events'; Per-item error handling: 'an `enrich` exception (LLM timeout/rate-limit) releases the claim'
findings.suggested_fix: In the per-item error handling paragraph, add: 'A `prune` failure after a successful `DONE_ENRICH` is logged and swallowed — the item is correctly done; prune will be retried on a subsequent drain pass when the DONE_ENRICH is seen without a pruned queue.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete default values for `RotatingFileHandler` maxBytes and backupCount in the plan (e.g., maxBytes=512_000, backupCount=2).
findings.finding: The `RotatingFileHandler` maxBytes and backupCount values are described only as 'bounded' with no concrete defaults, requiring the executor to choose arbitrary values.
findings.location: What section: 'configures a `logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler` (maxBytes+backupCount bounded)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Constants: `DRAIN_LOG_MAX_BYTES = 512_000`, `DRAIN_LOG_BACKUP_COUNT = 2`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a per-item wall-clock timeout for the `enrich()` call within the drain loop (or mandate that S1 exposes a timeout parameter used here), so a hung LLM call is eventually killed, the claim is released, and the batch continues.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no timeout for the `enrich` LLM call inside the drain child. The drain child process could hang indefinitely if the LLM endpoint stalls without returning an error, consuming a process slot and preventing the lock from being released until the process is killed externally.
findings.location: ## What — Drainer loop section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC or test coverage specifying that `rebar enrich status` outputs `{"pending":0,"claimed":0,"soaking":0}` (exit 0) when the queue store is absent, and outputs a non-JSON error message to stderr with a non-zero exit code on unrecoverable read errors.
findings.finding: The `rebar enrich status` subcommand specifies happy-path JSON output (`{\"pending\":int,\"claimed\":int,\"soaking\":int}`) but does not specify behavior for error/empty states: what is printed (and what is the exit code) when the queue store does not yet exist, is locked/unreadable, or returns a reducer error.
findings.location: ## What — CLI `rebar enrich` / `status` description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the `maybe_drain()` spec whether a parent-side spawn-guard (e.g., drain-lock-file existence check BEFORE Popen) is required to prevent burst-spawn under concurrent invocations, or explicitly state that spawning multiple children and letting the drainer lock sort it out is the accepted behavior.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `maybe_drain()` uses any spawn-guard (lightweight file flag or in-memory check) to prevent multiple concurrent parent invocations from each spawning a separate drain child after all passing the soaked?-check. The drainer-side lock handles double-processing, but the current spec would silently spawn N children for N near-simultaneous commands (e.g., a script calling `rebar` in a loop), each incurring subprocess overhead before the lock kicks in. The plan must clarify whether this double-spawn is accepted behavior (let the lock sort it out) or whether a lightweight parent-side guard (e.g., a lock-file existence check before Popen) is expected.
findings.location: ## What — `maybe_drain()` gate; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'maybe_drain injection point' section: 'Before spawning, `maybe_drain` also checks for the drain-lock file's existence as a cheap heuristic (not a guarantee) so that back-to-back invocations within the same second do not each pay the Popen cost; the drainer-side lock remains the authoritative concurrency guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `DRAIN_LOG_MAX_BYTES` and `DRAIN_LOG_BACKUP_COUNT` to the Constants section with concrete defaults (e.g., 1 MB and 3 files).
findings.finding: The plan's Constants section lists `DRAIN_BATCH = 5` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20` with defaults, but the RotatingFileHandler bounds (`maxBytes`, `backupCount`) are referenced only as 'bounded' without concrete defaults. Since disk growth is a stated concern (the Observability section says 'maxBytes+backupCount bounded'), implementers need a specified default to avoid divergent implementations and to make the log-growth bound verifiable.
findings.location: ## What — Observability; ## Constants
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Constants: `DRAIN_LOG_MAX_BYTES = 1_048_576` (1 MB), `DRAIN_LOG_BACKUP_COUNT = 3` — total max `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log*` footprint ≤ 4 MB.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing: (a) the opportunistic-detach pattern rationale, (b) the store→llm coupling decision and its chosen boundary (direct import vs. callback/event), and (c) the 'single injection point in event_append' constraint.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (opportunistic detached drain piggybacking ordinary write commands) AND a new cross-layer coupling (store layer `event_append.py` calling into LLM layer `enrich_drain.py`), but specifies no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc artifact to capture the decision rationale, the layer-boundary choice, or the trade-offs.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` → `src/rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py` injection
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0XXX-opportunistic-drain.md` (or append to CLAUDE.md) documenting: why event_append is the injection site, why a direct import rather than an event/callback boundary was chosen, and how to opt out or extend the drain batch size.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a thin boundary for the `maybe_drain` hook (e.g., a `Callable[[], None]` injected into `event_append` at startup, or a lightweight callback registered via a module-level setter) so the store layer has no hard import dependency on the llm layer.
findings.finding: The store→llm coupling (`event_append.py` calling `maybe_drain` from `enrich_drain.py`) is acknowledged and justified but no interface or event boundary is described as mitigation — it is a direct cross-layer import with no abstraction seam.
findings.location: 'maybe_drain injection point (decided)' section; Scope: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In `event_append.py`, accept an optional `drain_hook: Callable[[], None] | None = None` parameter (or a module-level `register_drain_hook(fn)` setter); the llm layer registers `maybe_drain` at application-init time; the store layer calls it if set. This keeps the import graph acyclic and makes the hook trivially mockable in store-layer tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit implementation note requiring that `subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS` and `subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW` are accessed only via `getattr(subprocess, 'DETACHED_PROCESS')` or strictly inside the `if os.name == 'nt':` block, and add a POSIX smoke-import test (or CI check) to confirm the module imports cleanly on Linux without AttributeError.
findings.finding: subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS and subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW are Windows-only CPython constants that do not exist in the codebase today (zero search hits). While these are real stdlib constants, they are Partially-verified: the plan references them correctly and guards them in the nt branch, but no existing code exercises this path. If either constant is referenced outside the nt branch (e.g., a module-level attribute access or import), Python raises AttributeError on POSIX. The test (test_windows_detach_kwargs) mocks os.name but cannot catch an accidental out-of-branch reference at import time.
findings.location: What section — 'Detach, cross-platform' paragraph; AC line 'Detach is os.name-branched: POSIX start_new_session, Windows DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW (referenced only in the nt branch)'
findings.suggested_fix: In enrich_drain.py, access the Windows constants lazily: `flags = getattr(subprocess, 'DETACHED_PROCESS', 0) | getattr(subprocess, 'CREATE_NO_WINDOW', 0)` inside the `os.name == 'nt'` branch only, matching the pattern used in acli_subprocess.py for start_new_session. Add a `test_posix_import` test that imports enrich_drain on a POSIX platform and asserts no AttributeError.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare a redaction/sanitisation posture for exception text logged to `enrich-drain.log` on the outbound LLM error path (e.g., wrap SDK exceptions before logging to strip or truncate HTTP headers, or explicitly state that SDK exceptions are logged only at a safe summary level that excludes request headers).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that drain failure exceptions are logged to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` (stderr redirected there) but does not declare any redaction posture for exception text that may include LLM SDK HTTP details (e.g., Authorization headers in tracebacks from the outbound LLM API boundary). This is an undeclared access posture on the logging path of a boundary-crossing component.
findings.location: Observability section: 'the child configures a logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler … writing to .rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log … redirects its own stderr there; drain failures stay diagnosable.'
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich_drain.py`, catch LLM exceptions and log only `type(exc).__name__` + a safe message string (not `repr(exc)` or the full traceback) to the rotating log handler. Full tracebacks may go to a DEBUG level that is off by default. Add a note to the plan: 'Exception logging on the LLM error path logs exception type and a sanitised message only — no repr/traceback that could embed HTTP Authorization headers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Demote `DRAIN_BATCH` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` from user-configurable `LLMConfig` fields to module-level constants in `enrich_drain.py` (with optional env-var override if tunability is needed), until a second consumer exists that justifies the shared config surface.
findings.finding: `DRAIN_BATCH` (default 5) and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` (default 20) are proposed as user-configurable LLMConfig fields (`overlap_drain_batch`, `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`) with only one planned consumer (`enrich_drain.py`). No grep hits show any existing or second planned consumer of these fields. Adding two tunable config knobs for a single internal component violates Rule-of-Three and adds unnecessary config surface that operators will rarely (if ever) touch.
findings.location: ## Constants (defaults stated) and ## Scope — `overlap_drain_batch`/`overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` on `LLMConfig`
findings.suggested_fix: Define `DRAIN_BATCH = int(os.environ.get('REBAR_LLM_DRAIN_BATCH', '5'))` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = int(os.environ.get('REBAR_LLM_DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS', '20'))` as module-level constants in `enrich_drain.py`. Remove `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` from `LLMConfig` and the Scope section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
